# John 11: The Resurrection and the Life

When the sisters of Lazarus send word to Jesus that their beloved brother is sick, they appeal to Him with the confidence of those who know they are loved: "Lord, the one You love is sick." Their message is simple, urgent, and assumes that Jesus will come immediately. Instead, Jesus stays where He is for two more days. Only then does He tell His disciples, "Let us go back to Judea"—back to the very place where people had just tried to stone Him. This delay is not cruelty; it is deliberate. Jesus explicitly states that Lazarus's sickness "is for the glory of God, so that the Son of God may be glorified through it." Something far greater than the healing of a sick man is at stake. Jesus is not merely reacting to a crisis but orchestrating a moment that will reveal who He truly is. The delay itself becomes an act of love, though it requires faith from those who cannot yet see what Jesus sees.

When Jesus finally arrives in Bethany, Lazarus has been dead for four days—long enough that the body would be decomposing, long enough that any hope of natural recovery is gone. Martha meets Him with a statement that contains both faith and accusation: "Lord, if You had been here, my brother would not have died. But even now I know that God will give You whatever You ask of Him." Then comes the statement that anchors the entire chapter. Jesus says to her, "I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in Me will live, even though he dies. And everyone who lives and believes in Me will never die. Do you believe this?" This is not a promise about future resurrection as an abstract doctrine. Standing before the tomb of a dead man, Jesus claims to be the power of life itself. He is not merely offering resurrection as a future event; He is the resurrection in the present moment. Martha's belief in Jesus becomes the only foundation solid enough to stand on when death has done its worst.

When Jesus sees Mary and the mourning crowd weeping over Lazarus's death, something extraordinary happens. The text tells us that Jesus was "deeply moved in spirit and troubled," and then, without explanation or commentary, "Jesus wept." He does not weep because He is uncertain about what He is about to do. He weeps because He stands before the reality of human death and grief, and He grieves with those who grieve. His tears are real, His compassion real, His identification with our suffering real—even as He is about to demonstrate that He alone holds the power over death. When He calls Lazarus from the tomb with the command "Lazarus, come out!"—the dead man walks out, bound in grave clothes. The miracle is undeniable, witnessed by many, and it crystallizes the choice before everyone who sees it: to believe that Jesus is who He claims to be, or to reject Him. The Sanhedrin's response shows what the real issue is. They do not argue that the miracle did not happen; they argue that if they allow Jesus to continue, everyone will believe in Him, and Rome will respond. In their fear, they plot to kill the very source of life.

What John's account forces us to confront is this: belief in Jesus is not a comfortable addition to life as we know it. Martha must believe in the resurrection while staring at her brother's tomb. We must believe in Christ as the resurrection and the life while we ourselves move toward death. The Sanhedrin's choice reveals that believing in Jesus often means choosing Him over security, reputation, and survival. The chapter ends with Jesus withdrawn from public view, hunted by the authorities, heading toward the very death that would make His claim to be "the resurrection and the life" the most severe test imaginable. He does not retreat because He is uncertain. He moves toward the cross because He is absolutely certain. What the raising of Lazarus accomplishes is not the prevention of death but the revelation of the One who has conquered it. And that conquest will be completed not by raising others from the dead, but by rising Himself.
